description:
You are assisting a researcher who is tidying-up the results of several
        experimental runs.  
        
        What already exists  
        1. A directory /home/user/data_runs/ that contains three plain-text log
           files produced by a homemade data-collection script.  
           • /home/user/data_runs/run1.log  
           • /home/user/data_runs/run2.log  
           • /home/user/data_runs/run3.log  
        2. Each *.log file consists of one message per line.  Every line starts
           with a time-stamp in square brackets followed by a severity word
           (INFO, WARNING or ERROR) and then a free-form message.  Example line:  
              [2024-01-01 10:01:00] ERROR Failed to open file  

        The researcher only wants to know how many ERROR messages occurred in
        each run.  They would like a compact CSV summary and to keep that
        summary in its own directory.

        Your task  
        A. Count how many lines in each of the three log files contain the exact
           word “ERROR”.  
        B. Create the directory /home/user/analysis_results/ if it does not
           already exist (it should end up with default 755 permissions).  
        C. Write a new file /home/user/analysis_results/error_summary.csv that
           follows *exactly* the format below:  

              file,count
              run1.log,2
              run2.log,1
              run3.log,3

           Formatting rules that the automated checker will enforce:  
           • The first row is the fixed header “file,count”.  
           • One subsequent row per log file in **alphabetical order by file
             name**.  
           • Fields are separated by a literal comma (“,”) with **no spaces**.  
           • Each line, including the last, is terminated by a single UNIX line
             feed (LF, “\n”).  No carriage returns.  
           • Do not include any extra rows or columns.  

        D. After creating the CSV file, display its contents to the terminal so
           the researcher can see the result immediately.

        A typical solution can be achieved in three or four simple shell
        commands (e.g., grep, wc, echo, etc.), but you may use any tools that
        are already present in a standard Ubuntu environment.  Do not modify
        the original *.log files.  The only new artefact on disk must be
        /home/user/analysis_results/error_summary.csv.
truth:
Pre-existing files and their exact contents (all lines end in LF):

        1. /home/user/data_runs/run1.log
           [2024-01-01 10:00:00] INFO Starting run
           [2024-01-01 10:01:00] ERROR Failed to open file
           [2024-01-01 10:02:00] WARNING Low memory
           [2024-01-01 10:03:00] ERROR Timeout occurred
           [2024-01-01 10:04:00] INFO Run finished

        2. /home/user/data_runs/run2.log
           [2024-02-01 11:00:00] INFO Starting run
           [2024-02-01 11:15:00] ERROR Invalid parameter
           [2024-02-01 11:30:00] INFO Run finished

        3. /home/user/data_runs/run3.log
           [2024-03-05 09:00:00] INFO Starting run
           [2024-03-05 09:05:00] WARNING Disk almost full
           [2024-03-05 09:10:00] INFO Still running
           [2024-03-05 09:15:00] ERROR Segmentation fault
           [2024-03-05 09:20:00] ERROR Failed to allocate memory
           [2024-03-05 09:25:00] ERROR Unknown exception
           [2024-03-05 09:30:00] INFO Run finished

        Expected artefact created by the agent:

        • /home/user/analysis_results/error_summary.csv  
          file,count\n
          run1.log,2\n
          run2.log,1\n
          run3.log,3\n

          (That is: four LF-terminated lines, exactly as shown.)

        The automated test will confirm:  
        1. Directory /home/user/analysis_results/ exists.  
        2. The CSV file exists, is readable, and its byte-for-byte content
           matches the block above.  
        3. The agent did not alter or delete any file inside
           /home/user/data_runs/.
